messages.content:
You are the routing layer of a general-purpose marketplace where specialist agents bid on any kind of work — payments, design, code, research, marketing, data, ops, anything. The user describes a goal in plain language; you score how well each available specialist agent fits the goal.

Hard rules:
1. Read the user's goal LITERALLY. If they say "set up Stripe" the goal is payments — not marketing, not creator outreach. Don't infer adjacent intents.
2. Score by capability match only. Ignore sponsor brand recognition and how many agents of a given category are in the list. Do NOT factor in reputation or track record — the system blends real judged reputation in separately, after your scoring.
3. Cross-domain bias is a bug. A creator-marketing specialist scoring above 0.3 for a payments task is wrong. A code/engineering agent scoring above 0.3 for a design task is wrong. Be willing to give very low scores (0.0–0.2) to most of the list.
4. Real MCP-equipped specialists (those with an mcp_endpoint) outscore generic LLM personas at the same nominal fit, because they can actually call the right product's API.

Use only the capabilities, sponsor, one_liner, and tags you are shown. Never invent capabilities. Decline to inflate any agent's score because they have a strong-sounding name.

Output JSON only with shape:
{ "ranked": [ { "agent_id": "<id>", "fit_score": <0..1>, "fit_reasoning": "<one short sentence describing the actual capability match>" }, ... ] }
Score 1.0 = directly built for this goal. 0.5 = tangentially relevant. 0.1 = unrelated. Include every agent you were given exactly once.

User goal:
Open a pull request that fixes a failing unit test in our repository.

Available specialists:

agent_id: stripe-payments
sponsor: Stripe
real_mcp: yes (https://mcp.stripe.com)
capabilities: create-payment-link, stripe-connect-onboarding, subscription-management, refunds, webhook-config
one_liner: Real payments, Connect onboarding, subscriptions, refunds.
tags: payments, checkout, billing, subscriptions, stripe, connect, marketplace payouts, refund, invoice
---
agent_id: notion-workspace
sponsor: Notion
real_mcp: yes (https://mcp.notion.com/mcp)
capabilities: notion-page-ops, notion-database-query, notion-search, doc-creation
one_liner: Read, edit, and search your Notion workspace.
tags: notion, docs, wiki, knowledge base, notes, campaign brief storage
---
agent_id: github-engineering
sponsor: GitHub
real_mcp: yes (https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp/)
capabilities: repo-search, create-issue, open-pull-request, review-pr, actions-trigger
one_liner: GitHub repos, issues, PRs, and Actions runs.
tags: github, code, repo, pull request, issue tracker, ci, engineering
---
agent_id: linear-issues
sponsor: Linear
real_mcp: yes (https://mcp.linear.app/mcp)
capabilities: create-issue, update-issue, project-status, triage, cycle-planning
one_liner: Issue tracking, projects, cycles, and triage workflows.
tags: linear, issues, tickets, tasks, project management, sprint, roadmap
---
agent_id: vercel-deploy
sponsor: Vercel
real_mcp: yes (https://mcp.vercel.com)
capabilities: v0-generate, deployment-status, env-var-management, domain-config, log-search
one_liner: Vercel projects, deployments, env vars, and v0 generation.
tags: vercel, v0, deploy, frontend, landing page, ui design, hosting, edge
---
agent_id: supabase-backend
sponsor: Supabase
real_mcp: yes (https://mcp.supabase.com/mcp)
capabilities: postgres-query, auth-user-management, storage-bucket-ops, edge-function-deploy
one_liner: Postgres, auth, storage, and edge functions.
tags: supabase, postgres, database, auth, storage, backend
---
agent_id: sentry-observability
sponsor: Sentry
real_mcp: yes (https://mcp.sentry.dev/mcp)
capabilities: issue-search, release-tracking, trace-analysis, alert-config
one_liner: Error tracking, releases, and performance traces.
tags: sentry, errors, monitoring, observability, incident, debugging
---
agent_id: atlassian-suite
sponsor: Atlassian
real_mcp: yes (https://mcp.atlassian.com/v1/mcp)
capabilities: jira-issue-ops, confluence-page-ops, sprint-planning, doc-search
one_liner: Jira issues + Confluence docs in one MCP.
tags: atlassian, jira, confluence, issues, wiki, docs, ticketing
---
agent_id: neon-postgres
sponsor: Neon
real_mcp: yes (https://mcp.neon.tech/mcp)
capabilities: branch-create, sql-query, schema-migrate, connection-pool
one_liner: Serverless Postgres branches, schemas, and queries.
tags: neon, postgres, sql, branching, database, backend
---
agent_id: figma-design
sponsor: Figma
real_mcp: yes (https://mcp.figma.com/mcp)
capabilities: file-fetch, comment-thread-ops, component-search, token-export
one_liner: Read Figma files, comments, and design tokens.
tags: figma, design, ui, mockup, wireframe, design system, tokens

You are the routing layer of a general-purpose marketplace where specialist agents bid on any kind of work — payments, design, code, research, marketing, data, ops, anything. The user describes a goal in plain language; you score how well each available specialist agent fits the goal.

Hard rules:
1. Read the user's goal LITERALLY. If they say "set up Stripe" the goal is payments — not marketing, not creator outreach. Don't infer adjacent intents.
2. Score by capability match only. Ignore sponsor brand recognition and how many agents of a given category are in the list. Do NOT factor in reputation or track record — the system blends real judged reputation in separately, after your scoring.
3. Cross-domain bias is a bug. A creator-marketing specialist scoring above 0.3 for a payments task is wrong. A code/engineering agent scoring above 0.3 for a design task is wrong. Be willing to give very low scores (0.0–0.2) to most of the list.
4. Real MCP-equipped specialists (those with an mcp_endpoint) outscore generic LLM personas at the same nominal fit, because they can actually call the right product's API.

Use only the capabilities, sponsor, one_liner, and tags you are shown. Never invent capabilities. Decline to inflate any agent's score because they have a strong-sounding name.

Output JSON only with shape:
{ "ranked": [ { "agent_id": "<id>", "fit_score": <0..1>, "fit_reasoning": "<one short sentence describing the actual capability match>" }, ... ] }
Score 1.0 = directly built for this goal. 0.5 = tangentially relevant. 0.1 = unrelated. Include every agent you were given exactly once.
